text.title: GraphQL API Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every api-graphql artefact must obey. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Production GraphQL endpoint MUST accept only persisted queries (allowlisted hash). Ad-hoc query strings from the network are rejected.
rule.rationale: Open ad-hoc surface gives an attacker arbitrary query construction; persisted-queries shrink the surface to a known set.

rule.statement: Every 1-to-N resolver MUST use a DataLoader (or equivalent batch+cache). N+1 patterns where each parent triggers a child query are forbidden.

rationale.source: Facebook DataLoader original blog post + Apollo perf guidance

rule.rationale: Without DataLoader, a query for 100 parents triggers 100 child queries; p95 latency rises with parent count.

rule.statement: Gateway MUST enforce max_depth <= 10 AND max_complexity <= 1000 (or document the per-environment override). Unlimited queries are forbidden.

rationale.source: Apollo Router / GraphQL Yoga security guidance

rule.rationale: Deep nested queries are a DoS vector; complexity also bounds CPU/DB cost per request.

rule.statement: GraphQL errors MUST carry `extensions.problem` with RFC 7807 fields (type, title, status, traceId) mirroring REST error envelope.
rule.rationale: Clients mixing REST + GraphQL otherwise need two error parsers; the shared envelope keeps them unified.

rule.statement: Nullable scalars MUST be the default; non-null forces clients into overfetch to avoid runtime errors. Mandatory fields are explicit per use case.

rationale.source: GraphQL spec authoring guidance + Internal — schema-evolution audit

rule.rationale: Non-null is a permanent commitment in GraphQL; over-tightening at v1 forces breaking changes later when fields legitimately become optional.
